text: The surgeon places ports and begins a peritoneal incision approximately two centimeters from the horizontal line at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon enters the extraperitoneal or preperitoneal space, advancing through the initial incision made near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon encounters loose areolar tissue and uses cold scissors along with short bursts of energy to create a wide dissection plane, ensuring no major vessels are visible within the plane.
In robotic surgery, the dissection is performed horizontally, contrasting with the vertical approach typically seen in laparoscopic procedures.
The surgeon performs clearances in zone one, manipulating tissue within the designated area.
The surgical field shows the area where nerves can be encountered within zone one, which is known as the triangle of pain.
The surgeon enters the medial compartment of zone two, where the rectal branch of the inferior epigastric artery is first encountered, indicating correct dissection progress.

The surgeon continues dissection in the lateral pelvic wall region, aiming to reach three centimeters below the pubic arch to establish an adequate plane. The bony landmark known as the lighthouse will soon be visible, confirming the correct dissection path.

The surgeon mobilizes the superior leaf of the peritoneum, taking care to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels and prevent bleeding. The central dissection is performed, revealing the indirect inguinal hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies a short mouth and a long seed-like sac, proceeding to dissect it from the surrounding tissues within the anatomical triangle.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to ensure the gonadal vessels are safeguarded and the vas deferens remains uninjured during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom, with the iliac vessels visible deeper at the base. A tunnel is formed below the hernial sac, demonstrating careful dissection to avoid injury to these vessels.
The surgeon seeks additional clearance on the medial side, ensuring the vas deferens is clearly identified and preserved during the dissection.

The surgeon encounters tougher tissues to dissect along the lateral pelvic wall and within the triangle of doom, which are visually challenging areas in younger patients and cases of long-standing hernias.

The surgeon widens the small tunnel beneath the hernial sac, ensuring the vas deferens is safeguarded. The indirect hernial sac is dissected, leaving the last one or two centimeters undivided near the scrotum or apex.
The surgeon carefully avoids entering the scrotum, ensuring that the testicular vessels and pampiniform plexus are not encountered, while safeguarding all surrounding tissues.
The surgeon reveals the vas deferens, which has been carefully protected and preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon performs extensive blunt dissection and applies energy devices to separate tissues in the specified area, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the sac, carefully cutting through the tissue to separate it.
The surgeon carefully places an insertion while repeatedly confirming that the vas deferens remains uninvolved, ensuring its preservation during the procedure.
The surgeon employs a technique of holding and releasing tissue during dissection, ensuring careful manipulation and preservation of surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses the elasticity of the tissue as a visual cue for identification and dissection, manipulating the tissue to observe its stretch and recoil properties.

The surgeon reveals a white structure below the sac, with a layer of tissue on top, indicating that both the vas and its vasculature have been preserved. Further clearance is performed on the lateral side.
The surgeon develops the dissection plane, ensuring it extends not only unilaterally but also slightly to the opposite side for thoroughness.

The surgeon places a flat two-dimensional mesh into the extraperitoneal pouch created in the lateral pelvic wall, ensuring the surrounding tissues are adequately divided to prevent folding or buckling of the mesh.
The surgeon uses articulating instruments to maneuver the flat two-dimensional mesh into its perfect position within the extraperitoneal pouch.
The surgeon performs additional dissection around the mesh placed in the extraperitoneal pouch to prevent folding and ensure stability, even after deflation of the pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon checks the placement of the mesh to ensure it remains flat and properly positioned during dissection, preventing any folding from the top.
The surgeon performs additional dissection around the mesh, ensuring it sits comfortably and securely in the surgical site without folding.
The surgeon performs a camera flip maneuver, adjusting the laparoscopic camera angle from thirty degrees upward to thirty degrees downward.
The surgeon confirms that the mesh is adequately positioned and proceeds with dissection on the opposite side, ensuring proper placement and stability of the mesh.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the laparoscopic camera and its proximity to the tissues, optimizing the view for ongoing dissection and mesh placement.
The surgeon creates the extraperitoneal space, carefully navigating within the correct anatomical plane to avoid encountering vessels or nerves, minimizing the risk of injury.
The inferior epigastric vessels are prominently visible on the screen, clearly identifiable within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection within the extraperitoneal space, carefully navigating the tissue planes to avoid vessels and nerves.
The surgeon navigates within the medial compartment, identified as zone two, continuing the dissection process in this specific area.
The surgeon bisects tissue in the area where the obturator vessels are located, taking care to avoid damaging these vessels during the dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection at the medial border of the triangle of doom, carefully manipulating tissue in this anatomical region.
The surgeon performs gentle push and pull maneuvers to develop the tissue plane effectively, minimizing the use of electrocautery or other energy sources.
The surgeon navigates a plane composed of loose areolar tissue, where even minimal bleeding is visible and can obscure the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the fat tissue of the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating it from underlying structures.
The surgeon places mesh on the left side of the anterior abdominal wall for reinforcement, ensuring it integrates firmly with the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the mesh inside the pouch, ensuring it lies flat and smooth against the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon identifies areas requiring further dissection to ensure the mesh sits flat and securely on the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon positions the mesh behind the pubic bone, ensuring adequate space for it to sit securely.
The surgeon creates additional lateral space to prevent any folding of the mesh, ensuring it lies flat against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a Vicryl 3-0 suture to place interrupted sutures, securing the mesh in position over the hernia site, which may be direct, indirect, or femoral.
The surgeon begins the tricky part of the procedure, suturing and fixing the mesh onto the bone, ensuring secure attachment.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to perform medial fixation superiorly, following previous lateral and medial fixation steps, with the suture being fine enough to minimize postoperative pain.
The surgeon completes the closure of the peritoneum on the right-hand side of the screen, using sutures to secure the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs suturing using Y-Crate 3-0 sutures in a running fashion, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon performs an interlocking stitch technique, visibly tightening the sutures in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the successful apposition of the peritoneal layers using a Vycle 3-0 suture, showing the aligned tissue edges.
The surgeon applies additional running sutures to close the middle end of the peritoneal incision, ensuring the edges are approximated securely.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture to secure the medial end of the peritoneal incision, ensuring the suture remains tight and does not slip easily. Proximal sutures are placed to lock the suture effectively, eliminating the need for additional ties.
The surgeon begins closing the indirect herniated opening, preparing to suture the area.
The surgeon closes the deep ring and peritoneum using sutures, ensuring the anatomical structures are securely apposed.

The surgeon completes the fixation on the right side and begins fixation on the left side, starting at the inferior angle. The approach varies based on anatomical convenience and site-specific considerations.
The surgeon performs fixation at the bone, carefully inserting the needle to take a bite of the periosteum, ensuring the mesh remains immobile. Sutures are placed on the soft tissue.
The surgeon forms a loop or lasso on the current side, utilizing a specific technique for securing the surgical site.
The surgeon begins suturing from the lateral angle towards the medial angle on the left side, following the anatomical contours for fixation.
The surgeon applies extra push-pull manipulation to the peritoneum at the medial end, allowing more freedom of movement compared to the lateral angle.
The surgeon secures the lateral angle of the peritoneum using a suturing technique, preparing to move medially.
The surgeon repeats the closure technique on the opposite side, removing tissue laxity and applying an interlocking suture to ensure a taut closure.
The surgeon takes a few more running suture bites along the incision line, preparing to conclude the surgical procedure.
